name: 3D Geometry
description: Explore shapes, surfaces, volumes and transformations in three dimensions

modules.title: Foundations of 3D Space
modules.description: Build intuition for 3D shapes: vertices, edges, faces and the coordinate system

chapters.title: Introduction to 3D Shapes
chapters.summary: Understand how 3D shapes differ from 2D, identify vertices, edges and faces, and explore the 3D coordinate system.

data.markdown: In 2D geometry we work with flat shapes — squares, circles, triangles. **3D geometry** adds a third dimension: depth. Instead of area, we now talk about **volume**. Instead of perimeter, we measure **surface area**. Every solid object around you — a box, a ball, a tin can — is a 3D shape.

steps.title: 1D: A line
steps.body: One dimension — only length. Like a wire stretched straight.

steps.title: 2D: A flat shape
steps.body: Two dimensions — length and width. Like a drawing on paper. Has area.

steps.title: 3D: A solid
steps.body: Three dimensions — length, width and height (depth). Like real objects. Has volume and surface area.

config.label: A cube — drag to rotate and see all six faces

data.question: A football is an example of which type of shape?
data.hint: You can hold a football — it has depth.
data.explanation: A football occupies space in three dimensions, so it is a 3D shape (a sphere).

data.question: A square and a cube are both 2D shapes.
data.hint: A square is flat; a cube has depth.
data.explanation: A square is 2D (flat). A cube is its 3D counterpart — it has length, width AND height.

data.markdown:
Every 3D solid is made of three basic parts:
- **Vertex** (plural: vertices) — a corner point where edges meet
- **Edge** — a straight line where two faces meet
- **Face** — a flat surface of the solid

Counting these carefully reveals deep patterns, including Euler's famous formula.

config.label: Cube: 8 vertices, 12 edges, 6 faces — drag to explore
Order by number of faces (fewest first)
Order by number of faces (fewest first)

data.question: A cube has 8 vertices and 12 edges. How many faces does it have?
data.hint: Use Euler's formula: V − E + F = 2

data.label: Euler's formula — holds for all convex polyhedra

lessons.title: The 3D Coordinate System
lessons.subtitle: Locating points in space with (x, y, z)

data.markdown: In 2D we locate points with (x, y). In 3D we need a **third axis**, z, which points toward or away from us. Every point in 3D space has coordinates **(x, y, z)**. The three axes are mutually perpendicular and meet at the **origin** (0, 0, 0).

steps.title: x-axis
steps.body: Points left–right. Positive x is to the right.

steps.title: y-axis
steps.body: Points up–down. Positive y is upward.

steps.title: z-axis
steps.body: Points forward–backward (depth). Positive z comes toward you.

steps.title: Reading a 3D coordinate
steps.body: Point (3, 2, 5) is 3 units right, 2 units up, 5 units forward from the origin.

data.label: Distance between two points in 3D space

config.label: Drag to see how each vertex has a unique (x,y,z) position

data.question: What is the distance from the origin (0,0,0) to point (3, 4, 0)?
data.explanation: √(9 + 16 + 0) = √25 = 5. This is the 3-4-5 right triangle in the xy-plane.

chapters.title: Prisms & Cylinders
chapters.summary: Discover how prisms and cylinders are formed by extending a 2D shape along a straight path.

data.markdown: A **prism** is a solid with two identical, parallel faces called **bases**, connected by flat rectangular faces called **lateral faces**. The bases can be any polygon — triangle, square, hexagon, etc. The shape of the base names the prism.

config.label: Triangular prism — two triangular bases, three rectangular sides

steps.title: Triangular prism
steps.body: Bases are triangles. 5 faces, 9 edges, 6 vertices.

steps.title: Rectangular prism (cuboid)
steps.body: Bases are rectangles. 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 vertices.

steps.title: Hexagonal prism
steps.body: Bases are hexagons. 8 faces, 18 edges, 12 vertices.

data.question: A pentagonal prism has how many faces?
data.explanation: 2 pentagonal bases + 5 rectangular lateral faces = 7 faces total.

lessons.title: Nets of Prisms
lessons.subtitle: Unfolding a solid into a flat shape

data.markdown: A **net** is what you get when you unfold a 3D solid so all its faces lie flat in a single plane. Nets are crucial for understanding surface area — the total area of all faces equals the area of the net. A cube has 11 different valid nets!

config.label: Watch the net fold into a cube

data.question: The area of a net equals the surface area of the 3D solid.
data.hint: The net contains exactly all the faces.
data.explanation: When you unfold a solid into a net, no faces are added or removed, so the areas are equal.

data.question: A cube has how many different valid nets?
data.hint: This is a famous combinatorics result.
data.explanation: A cube has exactly 11 distinct nets — each a different arrangement of 6 squares that folds into a cube.

data.markdown: A **cylinder** is like a prism but with circular bases. It has two circular faces (top and bottom) and one curved lateral surface. If you unroll the lateral surface, it forms a rectangle with width = circumference of the circle.

config.label: Every horizontal cut through a cylinder is a circle

data.question: Every cross-section of a cylinder parallel to its base is a circle.
data.hint: Think about slicing a can of beans horizontally.
data.explanation: Because the bases are circles and the sides are straight, any horizontal cut produces a circle of the same radius.

chapters.title: Pyramids, Cones & Spheres
chapters.summary: Meet the pointed and curved solids — pyramids, cones and spheres.

lessons.title: Pyramids
lessons.subtitle: A base that tapers to a single apex

data.markdown: A **pyramid** has a polygon base and triangular faces that meet at a single point called the **apex**. Like prisms, the base shape names the pyramid. The Great Pyramid of Giza is a square pyramid.

config.label: Square pyramid — drag to see all 5 faces

steps.title: Square pyramid
steps.body: 1 square base + 4 triangular faces = 5 faces. V=5, E=8, F=5.

steps.title: Triangular pyramid (tetrahedron)
steps.body: All 4 faces are triangles. V=4, E=6, F=4. The simplest polyhedron.

steps.title: Slant height vs height
steps.body: The height h is the perpendicular distance from apex to base. Slant height l is the height of a triangular face.

config.label: Tetrahedron — every face is an equilateral triangle

data.question: A triangular pyramid (tetrahedron) has 4 vertices and 4 faces. How many edges does it have?

lessons.title: Cones
lessons.subtitle: A circular base tapering to an apex

data.markdown: A **cone** is like a pyramid but with a circular base. It has one circular face and one curved lateral surface that tapers to a point (apex). Ice cream cones and traffic cones are real-world examples. The **slant height** l is the distance from apex to any point on the base edge.

config.label: Slice a cone at different heights

data.label: Slant height l from radius r and perpendicular height h

data.question: A cone with radius 3 and height 4 has slant height:

data.markdown: A **sphere** is the set of all points in 3D space that are exactly **r** units from a central point. Every cross-section through the centre is called a **great circle**. A sphere has no flat faces, no edges and no vertices — it is perfectly smooth.

config.label: Cutting a sphere at different heights

data.question: All cross-sections of a sphere are circles.
data.hint: Any plane that cuts a sphere creates a circular boundary.
data.explanation: True — any planar cross-section of a sphere is a circle. The largest is the great circle through the centre.

data.question: Which cross-section of a sphere is the largest?
options: The great circle through the centre
One near the equator but not at the centre
data.answer: The great circle through the centre
data.hint: The closer to the centre, the larger the cross-section.
data.explanation: The cross-section through the centre has the full radius r, giving maximum area πr².

modules.title: Surface Area
modules.description: Calculate how much material covers the outside of any 3D solid

chapters.title: Surface Area of Prisms & Pyramids
chapters.summary: Use nets and formulas to find the total surface area of prisms and pyramids.

lessons.title: Surface Area of a Cuboid
lessons.subtitle: Adding up all six rectangular faces

data.markdown: The **surface area** of a solid is the total area of all its outer faces. For a cuboid with length l, width w and height h, there are three pairs of identical rectangular faces.

config.label: Unfold to see the six faces of a cuboid
SA of a cube: 6s² — adjust side length

data.question: A cuboid is 4 cm × 3 cm × 2 cm. What is its surface area in cm²?

lessons.title: Surface Area of a Triangular Prism
lessons.subtitle: Two triangles and three rectangles

data.markdown: A triangular prism has **2 triangular bases** and **3 rectangular lateral faces**. The surface area is the sum of all five faces. The key is finding the area of the triangular base first.

data.label: 2 triangular bases + 3 rectangles (a,b,c = triangle sides, L = prism length)

steps.title: Step 1: Area of both bases
steps.body: 2 × (½ × base × height_of_triangle)

steps.title: Step 2: Area of three rectangles
steps.body: Each rectangle = one side of the triangle × length of prism. Add all three.

steps.title: Step 3: Add them up
steps.body: SA = 2 × base area + lateral area

config.label: Identify all 5 faces of this triangular prism

data.question: A triangular prism has equilateral triangular bases (side 4 cm, height 3.46 cm) and length 10 cm. What is the lateral surface area?
data.explanation: 3 rectangles each of area 4 × 10 = 40 cm². Total lateral area = 120 cm².

data.markdown: For a pyramid, surface area = **base area** + **total lateral area** (the triangular faces). For a square pyramid, each lateral face is an identical isoceles triangle. The height of these triangles is the **slant height** l, not the pyramid's vertical height h.

data.label: s = base side, l = slant height

config.label: Square pyramid — see the 4 triangular lateral faces

data.question: A square pyramid has base side 6 cm and slant height 5 cm. Find the total surface area in cm².
data.hint: Base area = 36. Lateral area = 4 × ½ × 6 × 5 = 60. Total?

chapters.title: Surface Area of Curved Solids
chapters.summary: Apply π to find the surface area of cylinders, cones and spheres.

lessons.title: Surface Area of a Cylinder
lessons.subtitle: Two circles and a rolled rectangle

data.markdown: Unroll a cylinder: you get **two circles** (top and bottom) and one **rectangle** (the curved side laid flat). The rectangle's width equals the circumference of the circle (2πr). This gives us the surface area formula.

config.label: Lateral area 2πrh: fix r=3, vary h

data.question: A cylinder has radius 7 cm and height 10 cm. What is the total surface area? (Use π ≈ 22/7)

lessons.title: Surface Area of a Cone
lessons.subtitle: A circle base and a curved fan

data.markdown: Unroll a cone's lateral surface: it becomes a **sector** (fan-shape) of a circle. The radius of that sector equals the **slant height** l of the cone. The arc length of the sector equals the circumference of the cone's base (2πr).

data.label: Base circle area + lateral sector area

steps.title: Find slant height l
steps.body: If r and h are given: l = √(r² + h²)

steps.title: Base area
steps.body: πr²

steps.title: Lateral area
steps.body: πrl — the area of the unrolled sector

steps.title: Total SA
steps.body: πr² + πrl = πr(r + l)

data.question: A cone has radius 3 cm and slant height 5 cm. Find the total surface area in cm² (leave in terms of π).

data.markdown: Archimedes discovered that the surface area of a sphere equals exactly **four times** the area of its great circle. This is a stunning result: SA = 4πr². Archimedes was so proud of this that he asked for a sphere inscribed in a cylinder to be engraved on his tombstone.

data.label: Surface area of a sphere with radius r

config.label: SA = 4πr² — explore how SA grows with r

data.question: If a sphere's radius doubles, its surface area becomes:
data.hint: SA ∝ r². If r → 2r, then SA → 4r².
data.explanation: SA = 4πr². New SA = 4π(2r)² = 16πr² = 4 × original SA.

modules.title: Volume
modules.description: Master volume formulas for every major 3D solid

chapters.title: Volume of Prisms & Cylinders
chapters.summary: Volume = base area × height. Applies to all prisms and cylinders.

data.markdown: **Volume** measures how much 3D space a solid occupies. We measure it in **cubic units** (cm³, m³, etc.). The simplest way to understand volume: count how many 1×1×1 unit cubes fill the solid. For a 3×3×3 cube, that's 27 unit cubes.

config.label: Stack layers of unit cubes — Volume = l × w × h

data.question: A cuboid is 5 cm × 4 cm × 3 cm. What is its volume in cm³?

data.markdown: The universal rule for all prisms: **Volume = Base Area × Height**. This works because a prism is just a base shape stacked to height h. The shape of the base doesn't matter — just find its area and multiply by the height.

data.label: Applies to triangular, hexagonal, any prism

config.label: Triangular prism volume: base area × h (base area = 10 cm²)

data.question: A triangular prism has a right-triangle base with legs 6 cm and 8 cm, and the prism is 12 cm long. What is its volume in cm³?
data.explanation: Base area = ½ × 6 × 8 = 24 cm². V = 24 × 12 = 288 cm³.

lessons.title: Volume of a Cylinder
lessons.subtitle: πr²h — a circle stacked to height h

data.markdown: A cylinder is just a circle (base area = πr²) stacked to height h. So Volume = πr²h. This is the same rule as for prisms — base area × height.

data.label: Volume of a cylinder with radius r and height h

config.label: V = π × r² × h — vary r to see volume grow

data.question: A cylinder has radius 3 cm and height 7 cm. Volume = ? (π ≈ 22/7)

chapters.title: Volume of Pyramids, Cones & Spheres
chapters.summary: Why pyramids and cones hold exactly ⅓ as much as their prism/cylinder counterparts.

data.markdown: A remarkable fact: you can fit exactly **three identical pyramids** inside a prism of the same base and height. So the volume of a pyramid is exactly **⅓ × base area × height**. This was first proved by Cavalieri's principle.

config.label: Square pyramid — volume is ⅓ of a square prism of same base and height

data.question: A square pyramid has base 6 cm × 6 cm and height 9 cm. Volume = ? cm³

data.markdown: Just like pyramids, a cone holds exactly **⅓** the volume of a cylinder with the same base and height. So: V = ⅓πr²h. Next time you fill an ice cream cone from a cylindrical tub, remember: three ice cream cones fill the tub!

config.label: Cone volume ⅓πr²h (fix r=3): see effect of h

data.question: A cone and a cylinder share the same base and height. The cone's volume is:
data.hint: This is the fundamental ⅓ relationship.
data.explanation: V_cone = ⅓πr²h = ⅓ × V_cylinder. Always one-third.

data.markdown: Archimedes also proved that a sphere fits inside a cylinder (radius r, height 2r) and occupies exactly **⅔** of that cylinder's volume. This gives us V = (4/3)πr³. Notice that volume grows with r **cubed** — small increases in radius give large increases in volume.

data.label: Volume of a sphere — grows as r cubed

config.label: V = (4/3)π·r³ — how fast does sphere volume grow?

data.question: A sphere has radius 3 cm. Volume = ? (π ≈ 22/7)
If the radius of a sphere doubles, its volume becomes:
data.explanation: V = (4/3)πr³. New V = (4/3)π(2r)³ = 8 × original volume.

modules.title: Advanced 3D Geometry
modules.description: Euler's formula, Platonic solids, cross-sections, projections and 3D transformations

chapters.title: Polyhedra & Euler's Formula
chapters.summary: Discover the deep relationship V − E + F = 2 and meet the five Platonic solids.

lessons.title: Euler's Polyhedron Formula
lessons.subtitle: V − E + F = 2 for every convex solid

data.markdown: One of the most surprising results in geometry: for **any convex polyhedron**, if you count Vertices (V), Edges (E) and Faces (F), you always get V − E + F = **2**. This is called **Euler's formula** (1752). It doesn't matter how complex the solid — the formula always holds.

data.label: Euler's Polyhedron Formula — a topological invariant

config.label: Cube: 8 − 12 + 6 = 2 ✓ Drag to verify faces/edges/vertices
Order shapes by number of vertices (fewest first)
Order shapes by number of vertices (fewest first)

data.question: A polyhedron has 20 vertices and 30 edges. Using Euler's formula, how many faces does it have?
data.explanation: 20 − 30 + F = 2 → F = 2 − 20 + 30 = 12. This is the dodecahedron!

data.markdown: A **Platonic solid** is a polyhedron where every face is the same regular polygon and every vertex has the same number of faces meeting at it. Remarkably, there are **exactly five** such solids — proved by Euclid in 300 BCE. The ancient Greeks associated them with the four elements plus the cosmos.

steps.title: Tetrahedron
steps.body: 4 equilateral triangle faces, 4 vertices, 6 edges. Associated with Fire.

steps.title: Cube (Hexahedron)
steps.body: 6 square faces, 8 vertices, 12 edges. Associated with Earth.

steps.title: Octahedron
steps.body: 8 equilateral triangle faces, 6 vertices, 12 edges. Associated with Air.

steps.title: Dodecahedron
steps.body: 12 regular pentagon faces, 20 vertices, 30 edges. Associated with the Cosmos.

steps.title: Icosahedron
steps.body: 20 equilateral triangle faces, 12 vertices, 30 edges. Associated with Water.

config.label: Tetrahedron — the simplest Platonic solid

data.question: How many Platonic solids exist?
data.hint: Euclid proved this is exact — not 4, not 6.
data.explanation: Exactly 5 Platonic solids exist: tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron.

data.question: All Platonic solids satisfy Euler's formula V − E + F = 2.
data.hint: Euler's formula holds for ALL convex polyhedra.
data.explanation: Yes — every Platonic solid is a convex polyhedron, so V − E + F = 2 for each.

chapters.title: Cross-Sections & Projections
chapters.summary: What 2D shapes appear when you slice or project 3D solids?

lessons.title: Cross-Sections of Solids
lessons.subtitle: What shape appears when you slice a solid?

data.markdown: A **cross-section** is the 2D shape you see when you cut a 3D solid with a flat plane. The shape changes depending on the solid and the angle of the cut. Cross-sections are essential in architecture, engineering and medical imaging (MRI scans are cross-sections of the body).

config.label: Slice a cone horizontally at different heights

steps.title: Cutting a cube horizontally
steps.body: Produces a square at every height.

steps.title: Cutting a cylinder horizontally
steps.body: Always produces a circle of the same radius.

steps.title: Cutting a cone horizontally
steps.body: Circle near the base, shrinking circle toward the apex — this gives Conic Sections.

steps.title: Cutting a cone at an angle
steps.body: Can produce ellipses, parabolas, or hyperbolas — the famous Conic Sections of Apollonius.

config.label: Horizontal cuts through a square pyramid

data.question: What cross-section does a horizontal cut through a sphere produce?
data.hint: Every point on a sphere is the same distance from its centre.
data.explanation: Any horizontal (or any flat) cross-section of a sphere is a circle.

lessons.title: Conic Sections
lessons.subtitle: Ellipses, parabolas and hyperbolas from a cone

data.markdown: When you slice a **double cone** at different angles, you get four classic curves: circle, ellipse, parabola and hyperbola — collectively called **Conic Sections**. They appear throughout nature and technology: planets orbit in ellipses, satellite dishes are parabolas, atomic particle paths are hyperbolas.

steps.title: Circle
steps.body: Cut perpendicular to the axis. All points equidistant from centre. x² + y² = r²

steps.title: Ellipse
steps.body: Cut at a slight angle. Two foci. x²/a² + y²/b² = 1. Planets orbit in ellipses (Kepler).

steps.title: Parabola
steps.body: Cut parallel to one slant edge. y = ax². All satellite dishes use this shape.

steps.title: Hyperbola
steps.body: Cut through both cones. Two branches. x²/a² − y²/b² = 1. Used in GPS and navigation.

config.label: Parabola y = ax² — adjust a to see curvature change

data.question: Planets orbit the Sun in which type of conic section?
data.explanation: By Kepler's First Law (1609), every planet orbits the Sun in an ellipse with the Sun at one focus.

chapters.title: 3D Transformations & Composite Solids
chapters.summary: Reflect, rotate and combine 3D shapes to solve complex real-world problems.

data.markdown: In 3D, symmetry is more complex than in 2D. A 3D shape can have **planes of symmetry** (like a mirror) and **axes of rotation** (spin around a line). A sphere has infinite planes of symmetry. A cube has 9 planes of symmetry and 13 axes of rotation.

config.label: Rotate the cube — find its symmetry axes

steps.title: Planes of symmetry
steps.body: A plane that divides a solid into two mirror-image halves. A cube has 9 planes of symmetry.

steps.title: Rotational symmetry
steps.body: Spin the shape around an axis — if it looks the same after less than 360°, it has rotational symmetry.

steps.title: Order of rotational symmetry
steps.body: A square face of a cube has order-4 rotational symmetry (looks same after 90°, 180°, 270°, 360°).

data.question: How many planes of symmetry does a cylinder have?
data.hint: Think about cutting a cylinder through its central axis at any angle.
data.explanation: Any plane through the central axis creates two mirror halves. Since the axis can be rotated to any angle, there are infinitely many planes of symmetry.

lessons.title: Composite Solids
lessons.subtitle: Combining shapes to solve real-world problems

data.markdown: Real-world objects are often **composite solids** — combinations of simpler shapes. A pencil is a cylinder with a cone on top. A silo is a cylinder with a hemisphere on top. To find the surface area or volume of a composite solid, **break it into parts** and add (or subtract) carefully.

steps.title: Step 1: Identify the parts
steps.body: Sketch the solid and label each component shape.

steps.title: Step 2: Note shared surfaces
steps.body: Surfaces where two parts join are NOT part of the outer surface — subtract them.

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate each part
steps.body: Find SA or volume of each component separately.

steps.title: Step 4: Combine
steps.body: Add volumes. For SA: add exposed faces only, not internal joins.

config.label: Silo volume: cylinder part πr²h (r=4, vary h)

data.question: A toy is made of a cylinder (r=3, h=8) topped by a hemisphere (r=3). What is the total volume? (π ≈ 3.14)

config.label: Order the steps to find volume of a composite solid
Order the steps to find volume of a composite solid
